entry.title: Levenshtein distance algorithm
entry.content:
Fuzzy Search and Levenshtein Algorithm

Have you ever wondered how similar two words are? How many changes do you need to make to turn one word into another? For example, how many letters do you need to swap to turn “Sowrd” into “Sword”? That’s the question that the Levenshtein Distance can answer. The Levenshtein Distance is a way of comparing how similar two words are. It counts how many changes you need to make to one word to get the other word. For example, to change “Male” to “Mace”, you only need to change one letter, so the Levenshtein Distance is 1. To change “Sowrd” to “Sword”, you need to change two letters, so the Levenshtein Distance is 2. This algorithm was invented by a Russian scientist named Vladimir Levenshtein. The store was part of a game that was aimed for 9–15-year-olds, so I had to make sure that the search function was robust and user-friendly. This meant that I had to handle cases where the users would misspell or omit letters in the words they were looking for. At first, I tried to simply match the input string with the weapon names in the store, but this approach was too rigid and failed to account for the variations in spelling. I realized that I needed a more sophisticated way to measure the similarity between two strings, and that’s when I discovered the Fuzzy Search and the Levenshtein Distance algorithm. Fuzzy search is a technique used in information retrieval and text searching to find results that are approximately, rather than exactly, matching a search query. It is particularly useful when dealing with data that may contain errors, misspellings, or variations in spelling and formatting. One of the fundamental algorithms used for fuzzy search is Levenshtein distance algorithm. The benefits of using Levenshtein distance algorithm is search result precision, customization of search and simplicity. When you compare two strings, the lower the Levenshtein Distance, the more similar the two strings are. I decided to use this algorithm as the basis for my search function, and I learned how to implement it in my code. This was a great opportunity for me to apply my computer science knowledge to a real-world problem and to improve my coding skills. Below I will detail my attempt at implementing the Levenshtein distance algorithm into my code:

local function levenshteinDistance(str1, str2)
    local len1, len2 = #str1, #str2
    local dp = {}

First, I define a function levenshteinDistance that takes in two parameters str1, and str2 which are both strings. It then assigns the variables len1 and len2 to the number of characters each string has. The # modifier calculates how many characters are within the strings that go through the function. For example, if the string sword was passed through the function, it would calculate that the length of that string is 5 The function then defines a table call dp which is a common variable name used in the establishment of the Levenshtein algorithm.

for i = 0, len1 do
        dp[i] = {}
        for j = 0, len2 do
            if i == 0 then
                dp[i][j] = j
            elseif j == 0 then
                dp[i][j] = i
            else

This code creates a table with rows and columns that represent parts of the sequences. Each cell in the table shows how many operations are needed for those parts. The code fills in the table by starting from the top left corner and moving right and down. It uses simple rules to calculate the values in the table based on the letters at the current positions and the values in the previous cells. The value at dp[i][j] represents the Levenshtein distance between the first i characters of s1 and the first j characters of s2.

local cost = str1:sub(i, i) ~= str2:sub(j, j) and 1 or 0
                dp[i][j] = math.min(dp[i - 1][j] + 1, dp[i][j - 1] + 1, dp[i - 1][j - 1] + cost)
            end
        end
    end

    return dp[len1][len2]
end

We iterate through the matrix to fill it in, using the recurrence relation for the Levenshtein distance: dp[i][j] is the minimum of three possible operations

Finally, the bottom-right cell of the matrix (dp[len_s1][len_s2]) contains the Levenshtein distance between the entire strings s1 and s2. I hope you enjoyed reading about Levenshtein distance algorithm and learned something new along the way.

```
entry.summary: Enhancing User Experience in Space Nights Store: Leveraging the Levenshtein Distance Algorithm for Precise Spell Check Solutions

entry.title: Level Creation for Roblox Game
entry.content:
Designing a level

Designing a level for a game on the Roblox platform was a challenging task for me. I initially thought that creating a level is to add different buildings in different locations. However, I soon realized that there were many factors to consider when creating a level that would attract and retain players. One of the most important aspects of level design was the placement of buildings. I had to decide where to put each building, how many of them to use, and what orientation they should have. I also had to make sure that the buildings made sense in the context of the game world and the gameplay. For example, I had to place the hospital, the storage centers, the city building and the power generators in locations that were logical and convenient for the players. I also had to align them with the roads and other elements of the environment. I experimented with different layouts to test the effect on the player experience. I had to consider things like visibility, accessibility, navigation, interaction, and immersion. The challenge was the player’s navigation. They should be able to see the buildings clearly, reach them easily, move around them smoothly, interact with them meaningfully, and feel immersed in them in a realistic way.

Above is the before pic of one of the sections made for the game.

Once I had the layout done ,I shared it with other players and testers. I got valuable feedback that helped me improve my design. They liked the variety of buildings, the aesthetic appeal, and the gameplay balance of the level.They pointed out issues that I had overlooked, such as collision detection, lighting effects, sound effects, and performance optimization.

Above is the after pic of one of the sections made for the game after taking in the feedback offered by my peers.

This journey taught me about player retention and level design. I learned how to balance aesthetics and functionality, how to create a coherent and immersive game world, and how to use feedback and testing to improve my work. I also learned how to use the Roblox platform effectively, as it offered many tools and resources that helped me create my level. I learned how to use the Roblox Studio editor, the Roblox scripting language Lua, the Roblox asset library, and the Roblox community forums.

```
entry.summary: Enhancing User Experience in Space Nights Game: Creating an intresting enviorment for players to expereince

entry.title: My Experience With Board Games
entry.content:
–

How Board Games Shaped My Life

Board games are more than just a hobby for me. They are a way of life. Ever since I was a kid, I have been fascinated by the different worlds and stories that board games can create. I have a closet full of board games, and each one has a special meaning for me. They have taught me valuable lessons, inspired me to pursue my passions, and challenged me to grow as a person. In this blog post, I will share some of the board games that have shaped my life and how they have influenced me.

Dinosaur Island: A Lesson in Persistence and Perseverance

Jean shorts raw denim Vice normcore, art party High Life PBR skateboard stumptown vinyl kitsch. Four loko meh 8-bit, tousled banh mi tilde forage Schlitz dreamcatcher twee 3 wolf moon. Chambray asymmetrical paleo salvia, sartorial umami four loko master cleanse drinking vinegar brunch. Pinterest DIY authentic Schlitz, hoodie Intelligentsia butcher trust fund brunch shabby chic Kickstarter forage flexitarian. Direct trade cold-pressed meggings stumptown plaid, pop-up taxidermy. Hoodie XOXO fingerstache scenester Echo Park. Plaid ugh Wes Anderson, freegan pug selvage fanny pack leggings pickled food truck DIY irony Banksy.

Charterstone: A Lesson in Community Building

Charterstone is a game where you have to build your own village in a fantasy world. You have to collect resources, construct buildings, and unlock new features as you play. The game is very immersive and creative, and I loved it from the first time I played it. I loved building my own manufacturing centers using the materials provided by the game, which reflected my passion for innovation. I also loved the suspense of unlocking crates to get items that would enhance my village.

However, I made a mistake in this game. I was so focused on my own village that I ignored what other players were doing. I did not realize that every decision I made would affect the whole community. Each move had a consequence. For example, if I used up all the resources in one area, other players would not be able to use them. Or if I built a building that gave me an advantage, other players would lose out on it. By the end of the game, my village had the lowest points, and I felt disappointed.

This game taught me that community building is not just about myself, but about others as well. It taught me that I need to be aware of the needs and interests of other people, and work together with them for the common good. It taught me that cooperation is better than competition. So, I decided to change my attitude in real life. I started to look for opportunities to make a positive impact on my community. I started to volunteer at Forgotten Harvest, a local food bank that helps underprivileged families. There, I helped sort and pack food items, distribute them to the needy, and raise awareness about food insecurity issues. I have volunteered more than 100 hours so far, and have received the Presidential medal for my volunteer work. I plan to continue my activities to uplift my community.

Cube Defenders: A Lesson in Creativity and Feedback

Cube Defenders is a game that I created myself. It is a tower defense game where you have to protect your base from waves of enemies using different types of cubes with special abilities. You can also upgrade your cubes and unlock new ones as you play. Creating this game was a dream come true for me. It was something that I always wanted to do since I was a kid.

Creating this game was not easy though. It required me to brainstorm, build, destroy, and design the game from scratch. It required me to research probability concepts and game theory fundamentals to improve the game playability. It required me to test and debug the game multiple times until it worked properly.

I faced many challenges along the way, such as failed map terrains, mismatched enemy powers, unclear instructions, and boring gameplay. But the biggest challenge was getting feedback from other people. When I first showed my game to my family, they did not enjoy it at all. They found it too hard or too easy or too confusing. They did not understand the rules or the goals or the strategies. They did not have fun with it.

This experience taught me the valuable lesson that I need to present a prototype early, so that I can get feedback from potential players and improve my game accordingly. I learned that feedback is essential for any creative project, and that I need to listen to it and act on it. I learned that feedback is not a criticism, but a way to make my game better.

I applied this lesson later in life when I did an internship at a video game company. There, I was assigned to create a “weapons shop” for a new game. I developed a prototype with various designs and user experience elements. I then invited my friends and peers to test it and give me feedback. I incorporated their feedback into my development, and was able to deliver a much better shop with added features. This boosted my confidence, and made me proud of my work.

Conclusion

Board games are not just pieces of cardboard and plastic, but windows into different worlds and stories. They are not just games, but teachers. They have shaped my life in many ways, and have influenced me in many aspects. They have taught me persistence, perseverance, community building, creativity, and feedback. They have inspired me to pursue my passions, and challenged me to grow as a person. They have made me who I am today, and I hope they will continue to do so in the future.

–
entry.summary: A blog about some board games i have played
